flashcards.front: What is the fundamental principle of a queue data structure?
flashcards.back: FIFO - First In, First Out. The element inserted first is the first one to be removed, like a real-world queue at a ticket counter.

flashcards.front: What are the two primary operations performed on a queue?
flashcards.back: ENQUEUE (or push/insert) - adds an element to the rear/tail. DEQUEUE (or pop/remove) - removes an element from the front/head.

flashcards.front: What is overflow condition in a queue?
flashcards.back: Overflow occurs when trying to enqueue an element into a queue that is already full, indicating no more space is available.

flashcards.front: What is underflow condition in a queue?
flashcards.back: Underflow occurs when trying to dequeue from an empty queue, indicating no elements are available to remove.

flashcards.front: What are the two ends of a queue called?
flashcards.back: FRONT (or head) - where elements are removed (dequeue). REAR (or tail) - where elements are added (enqueue).

A linear queue is a simple queue implementation where elements are arranged sequentially. It suffers from the limitation that even if the queue has space, you cannot add elements after the rear reaches the end of the array (phantom overflow).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of enqueue and dequeue operations in a queue?
flashcards.back: Both enqueue and dequeue operations have O(1) time complexity, as they only involve adding or removing from one end without iterating through the queue.
A priority queue is a variation where elements are removed based on priority rather than FIFO order. Higher priority elements are dequeued before lower priority ones.
A DEQUE allows insertion and deletion from both ends (front and rear). It combines the properties of both queue and stack.

flashcards.front: What are the applications of queues in computing?
flashcards.back: CPU task scheduling, print job spooling, breadth-first search (BFS) in graphs, asynchronous data transfer, and handling requests in web servers.

flashcards.front: How is a queue represented using an array?
flashcards.back: Using a one-dimensional array with two pointers: front (index of first element) and rear (index where next element will be inserted). Elements are stored sequentially from front to rear.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between front and rear pointers in an empty queue?
flashcards.back: In an empty queue, both front and rear are typically set to -1 (or 0, depending on implementation). After first enqueue, both point to the same index.

flashcards.front: What is circular queue and why is it used?
flashcards.back: A circular queue connects the rear to the front, forming a circle. It efficiently uses space by reusing vacant positions at the front when the rear reaches the end, eliminating phantom overflow problem.
A queue implemented using a linked list with pointers to both front and rear nodes. It dynamically grows and shrinks, avoiding the size limitation of array-based queues.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of a queue implementation?
flashcards.back: Space complexity is O(n) where n is the maximum number of elements the queue can hold, as it requires storage for all elements.
